I have another real-world example in its documentation. Raw alignments in IGV and alignments after realignment are illustrated in accompanying images. One can find plenty of similar scenarios that result in SNPs rather than simply losing coverage for InDels.

instruction: Explicitly check whether all reads have an RG tag and whether the read groups are well-defined.
output: Run samtools stats --split RG <file1.bam> to obtain summary statistics separated by read group. If it does not produce a list of ungrouped reads, the counts/statistics will match those from running samtools stats without the --split RG argument. The --split command also creates .bamstat files for individual read groups, which can be examined for the raw total sequences. For instance, grep 'raw total sequences' *.bamstat; the sum of these should equal the total sequences reported by samtools stats. If the sums match, there are no ungrouped reads in the file.

instruction: Determine whether Pilon can be used to polish PacBio subreads and recommend an alternative if not.
output: Use Canu for read-level correction instead of Pilon. Canu's latest version tracks all reads during correction and assembly. Ensure every raw read is corrected or used as evidence for correcting other reads, which helps rescue short plasmids in high coverage datasets and removes the need to set corOutCoverage. More details: https://github.com/marbl/canu/releases/tag/v1.7

instruction: Simulate Oxford Nanopore reads for Arabidopsis thaliana, including obtaining species‑specific training data, avoiding reference bias, and selecting appropriate tools for SV calling.
output: It is important to train an error model as NanoSim does, as we do not fully understand the error processes involved in both the nanopore sequencing process and the basecalling process. Any sort of read simulator that does not use a species‑specific model is just not going to produce realistic simulated reads. As far as datasets go, I would recommend looking at this assembly and the associated data uploaded on EMBL: Michael, Todd P., et al. 'High contiguity Arabidopsis thaliana genome assembly with a single nanopore flow cell.' Nature communications 9.1 (2018): 541. There are several data types there, as well as an assembly to train the models on. Is it OK to train a model using reads from one individual and a reference assembly from another individual? Generally, it is not good to map reads from one individual to the reference genome of another individual since this introduces something called reference bias. When we align reads to a reference genome generated from the same reads, or just from other reads from the same individual we remove this problem. All true reads will map to correctly assembled parts of the genome. Why can we train a model for nanopore reads on a reference generated from ... nanopore reads? Won't we get a bad model for indels? Yes, well, maybe. If the reference genome has been corrected to remove indels using a tool like pilon, then you can generally presume that the reference genome has very few to no indels present as a result of sequencing error from the Nanopore/PacBio reads. As a result, the model derived from aligning reads should have the correct error profile for indels. Again, it is important that the long‑read‑assembly was corrected with Illumina shotgun reads from the same individual from which the long reads were derived. Otherwise the corrected reference would have a chimaera of indels and SNPs from multiple individuals.

instruction: Identify a software or algorithm that treats gaps as indels when building a phylogenetic tree and describe how to use it.
input: nanopore sequencing, longread, NextStrain, IQTree, RAxML, PhyML

instruction: Explain what is meant by 'movie files' in PacBio sequencing.
output: Movie files are the PacBio raw data format, representing observed signal intensities in each sequencing well over the course of the sequencing run. They can be used to re‑call sequences with updated statistical models to improve accuracy or test hypotheses about how epigenetics alters the rate of nucleotide incorporation (e.g., methylation).

instruction: Explain whether Guppy 3 splits fast5 files into pass/fail folders when basecalling and whether you should use all fast5 files when re-basecalling with Guppy 4.4.
input: fast5 pass fail folders, guppy3, guppy4.4
output: Only if they've enabled the --qscore_filtering flag when using Guppy; this flag isn't enabled by default when running Guppy from the command line. It's a good idea to use all fast5 reads for recalling, because accuracy improves over time, so some that previously failed may slide into the pass folder. When recalling, you set the input and output directories (via the -i and -s flags). These should be different directories, and ideally different output directories for each recalling attempt, to avoid duplicate file entries. The original fast5 files shouldn't be modified by recalling as long as the input and output directories are different.

instruction: Use the provided script to split an indexed BAM file into reads containing a specified deletion and those without, generating two BAM files for downstream analysis.
output: I was asked to write some code to split reads based on variants at a particular location. The code creates SAM read groups based on those variants; the actual splitting can be done afterward with samtools split. Usage: samtools view -h mapped_reads.bam | ./samVarSplitter.pl [-ref <ref>] [-pos <int>] [options]. Example: samtools view -h mapped_reads.bam | ./samVarSplitter.pl -ref Wuhan-Hu-1 -pos 11083. If many reads do not include the variant location, pre-filter to reads that cover that location to reduce time. Note: the position argument currently supports only a single location; it can be extended to a range if needed.

instruction: Identify all occurrences of short sequences in long uncorrected reads.
input: bwa mem, blat, multiple sequence alignment
output: LAST has given the best results for me when I've tried to do this, although I agree with @user172818 that it's not a good idea to map really short reads. This is due to a combination of natural sequence duplication in long DNA sequences (e.g. see here), as well as abundant base calling differences present in single-molecule sequencing. Minimising error is not necessarily going to be the best option, and concentrating only on unique hits will miss a lot of signal. There are frequently multiple identically plausible positions, even at zero error, and the long reads will have their own associated errors. I also find the limitations a little odd. Canu can do read-level correction on long reads based on overlapping reads, and if assembly is possible, then nanopolish can be used to correct nanopore reads for systematic base-calling error introduced by the methylation of unamplified template.

instruction: Determine whether having a start position of 0 for all chromosomes in a BED file produced by a nanopore SV pipeline is normal.
output: The Python script opens the BAM file and iterates over each reference sequence. For each reference it prints the reference name, the start position 0, and the reference length. Thus the script deliberately outputs a start of 0 for every chromosome, using the zero‑based coordinate convention. The pipeline does not include coverage information; it only extracts the chromosome names and lengths.

instruction: Calculate the coverage of an RNA‑seq experiment with long‑read sequencing, considering uneven read length, a size‑fractionated protocol, and the choice of reads for N.
output: The read length is irrelevant when calculating the mean coverage statistic. It is simply the total number of bases sequenced divided by the target Xome length. In the example provided, L × N is equivalently expressed as σ⁻N l_x, the total length of sequenced reads. For equal‑length reads this is easier to calculate as number of reads times length of reads, but the full sum is necessary if read lengths are not equal. For what it's worth, this value, L × N, is also equivalent to the average read length multiplied by the number of reads. In answer to the second part of the question, N should ideally correspond to only the sequences that are from the reference. For an assembly, only the reads that were used to create the assembly should count when considering coverage. When there's no likelihood of contamination (and especially for RNA‑Seq with variable coverage per transcript), a statistic using the total number of reads should be good enough for most purposes. High‑throughput sequencing is such a fuzzy process that a few tens of millions of bases here or there shouldn't make a difference.

instruction: Call diploid SNV variants from long-reads data (~80 SMRT cells PacBio).
input: Canu, HaploMerger2, Falcon, FalconUnzip, HapCUT2, marginPhase, Clairvoyante
output: Try the longshot tool (https://github.com/pjedge/longshot) developed for calling variants in diploid genomes from long read data.

instruction: Identify a tool that uses concatemer information to improve base calling for plasmid sequencing data amplified with phi29 polymerase and sequenced on Oxford Nanopore.
output: Yes, there is a tool to do this called R2C2 by the Vollmers lab at UC Santa Cruz.

instruction: Determine whether stopping acquisition will negatively affect the results during a MinION sequencing run.
input: MinION, MinKNOW, phage lambda control, 48h run, 1 day 21 hours, no reads (Strand=0), single pores=0
output: Yes, you can click "stop acquisition" and your run won't be negatively affected. All of the reads are saved as they are generated. I am not sure how this will impact live basecalling though if that is something that you do.

instruction: Interpret the SMRT Link Base Modification Detection GFF3 output and determine where to find the modified base information, including why a CSV file might be missing.
output: It sounds like you are looking for the modifications.csv file, rather than the modifications.gff file. The .gff file is for viewing in SMRT View (or other viewers). The modified_base entries mean the algorithm could not determine the exact base and modification. The CSV file is also produced when motif analysis is not active, such as with the RS_Modification_Detection analysis protocol. It may be missing because the algorithm could not clearly identify any of the modifications, and you may need to contact tech support. The .gff file you posted includes significant hits (p < 0.01), but the algorithm was unable to classify them, likely because of insufficient coverage. Coverage recommendations for each event type are provided, with 100x being the lower bound.

instruction: Explain the meaning of the histogram numbers in MinKNOW, including how many reads have been produced and what the 4,120,722 number represents.
output: That's indeed the number of reads and that's quite low. How was your pore occupancy (number of pores sequencing) and your flow cell QC (number of pores good enough for sequencing)? How was your library concentration? Events is an approximation of nucleotides: it's a guess based on the current signal. As far as I know if you multiply this by 1.8 you get approximately your throughput in nucleotides. The 4,120,722 is the number of events in the currently selected (mouse hovering?) bin of the histogram.

instruction: Determine if ONT MinION sequencing can be used to detect structural variation and whether tools designed for PacBio, such as Sniffles, have been successfully employed; additionally, investigate if specific genomic regions can be targeted with MinION for detecting long inversions, duplications, etc.
input: Pindel, SVDetect, Manta, LUMPY, Sniffles, MinION
output:
A Structural Variant breakout session was held at the London Calling conference. The session notes are available in a summary by Constance Donnell. From those notes:
- Professor Wigard Kloosterman has developed a pipeline called NanoSV for mapping genomic structural variants in patients with congenital abnormalities.
- Tomas Sesani studied copy number variation of duplicated genes in viruses over time, but did not mention a public tool.
- Dr. Sudha Rao from Genotypic Technology in India used the MinION sequencer with Sniffles for structural variation calling.
ONT has indicated that the sessions and talks from London Calling 2017 will be made publicly available in the future.

instruction: Determine the best approach to normalize and compare treatment effects for AMR gene data from before and after treatment with no replicates.
output: Option a) Normalize to total read count, but this can perform poorly if there are compositional changes. Option b) Use DESeq2 normalization, which assumes most features do not change between samples. Option c) Normalize so that known features expected to remain constant are not differential.

instruction: Demultiplex MinION sequences with custom barcodes using available software or Albacore.
input: MinION, custom barcodes, Albacore, Nanopore Technologies
output: Use the Perl script fastq-dental.pl, which employs LAST for demultiplexing. Provide a barcode FASTA file and the input fastq.gz. The script trains a mismatch matrix, aligns reads to barcodes, and outputs separate files per barcode. Example command: ./fastq-dental.pl -barcode barcode_full_PBK004.fa -mat bc.mat reads_all.fastq.gz. The output lists read counts for each barcode. The barcode FASTA file can be replaced by any file containing barcode IDs. There is limited support for strand direction detection, but it has not yet been made to work for simultaneous demultiplexing and strand orientation. A protocol demonstrating the workflow is available at https://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.14egnxw4zl5d.

instruction: Compare the performance of Oxford Nanopore Flongle flow cells to standard MinION flow cells in terms of reliability, error rates, and suitability for metabarcoding with R10.4.1 nanopores and Kit 14 chemistry.
output: R10.4.1 Flongle flow cells give reduced yield because they have fewer working pores (about 10–20 compared with 30–60 for R9.4.1). The yield assumptions we use are: Flongle – 200 Mb, MinION – 5 Gb, PromethION – 50 Gb. With a rapid barcoding kit we can sequence and assemble 12 plasmids on one Flongle, and the improved accuracy from Kit 14 + R10.4.1 makes reliable assembly easier, especially with homopolymers. Error rates are similar across platforms because the pore determines the error profile, not the package. A comparison plot of mapped accuracy versus predicted accuracy (from the basecaller) shows that mapped quality is usually higher than predicted quality. The PromethION run produced 1,128,671 reads (1.95 Gb) over 1.5 h, while the Flongle run produced 465,144 reads (191 Mb) over 24 h. Predicted accuracy is calculated from the basecaller quality string. Base mismatch matrices indicate slightly lower accuracy for Flongle (99.9922 %) versus PromethION (99.9971 %), but the difference is not significant for practical purposes.

instruction: Find better pipelines/software for base‑calling that produce initial move tables so I can build on top of.
output: ONT’s latest basecaller Dorado can emit move tables in SAM output using the --emit-moves flag. Dorado is available on GitHub. An example command runs dorado basecaller with --emit-moves and pipes the output to samtools to create a BAM file. The move table appears in the mv tag as an array of 8‑bit signed integers. The model’s config.toml indicates a stride width of 5, so post‑processing will be needed to convert stride width to variable‑width events.

instruction: Find the coordinate on the opposite strand for a given genomic position using Tombo re-squiggling output.
input: h5py, Tombo, mapped_start, mapped_end, start, end, tombo_events, forward_seq, reverse_seq, start_idx
output: For reads mapped to + strand: start_idx = x- pos_mapped_start end_idx = y - pos_mapped_start + 1 required_signal= read_signal[start_idx: end_idx] For reads mapped to - strand: start_idx = neg_mapped_end - y- 1 end_idx = neg_mapped_end - x required_signal= read_signal[start_idx: end_idx] Note that x and y are inclusive and the length of the required signal = y - x + 1.

instruction: Describe what contributes to each single measurement in nanopore sequencing, whether it is a single nucleotide, a k‑mer, or the difference between the first and last base in the k‑mer, and explain how to determine k if the measurement reflects a k‑mer.
output: Sequencing channels measure electric current caused by ions flowing through the nanopore. The current is not measured per base directly; it reflects how the ion flow changes when different molecules occupy the pore. Older R9 pores could accommodate about five bases, newer R10 pores about ten. All bases inside the pore can influence the current, especially at the narrowest points of the pore. Unwinding of double‑stranded DNA can also affect the current profile a few bases before the pore. Therefore a 5/6‑mer model is reasonable because that is the number of bases that fit in the pore, and those bases have the greatest influence on the current. The measurement captures the combined effect of the bases present in the pore rather than a single nucleotide or only the difference between two bases.

instruction: Explain how to retrieve the duration of events in nanopore FAST5 files.
input: PacBio, Nanopore, IPD, raw signal, event, albacore basecaller, block stride, eventalign
output:
ONT no longer produce event data as default in their FAST5 output with the most recent basecallers, and they've been quite vague about how to generate it.
The easiest way to fix that is to use an older basecaller. If you tell Guppy v5.1.15 to output FAST5 files, it should include an event table that includes read start time and move information. This might also work with new Guppy, but ONT has told us that the event tables have gone away.
Processing the move tables to get information about signals per base is a bit complicated. Just using the metadata provided in the FAST5 files it's actually impossible, but you can get close enough to match what the ONT basecallers were trained on (which is probably close enough). To get closer than that, you need to process the raw signal yourself (e.g. see here).
Alternatively, you might be able to get that information out by following the tomboy documentation, which produces output that demonstrates signal-level changes:
https://github.com/nanoporetech/tombo
ONT have had various methods for basecalling over the years, and these methods change how the signal is chunked and processed.
When nanopore sequencing was first commercially available, base change points were resolved down to a single-sample level. Even with that approach, events were occasionally misclassified such that two consecutive events could represent the same base, or two consecutive events could involve a change of more than one base. To account for this, ONT created the move table, which described how many bases were shifted between one base and the next base. Initially ONT were using pentamers (i.e. 5 base sequences) for the output of their calling models, so the move table could have values of 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5.
Unfortunately, sample-level signal parsing is no longer the case, which makes precise event alignment tricky. Most recently, individual signal samples are aggregated into what are called strides, which describe the smallest length of time that the basecaller accepts as representing a single base transition.
Raw signal information is found at /baseLoc/../Raw/Signal. The stride length information is found in the block_stride attribute of /baseLoc/Summary/basecall_1d_template. The move table is found at /baseLoc/Move, and represents the number of base shifts in each stride block (usually either 0 or 1). It sounds like that move table is the statistic you're interested in; multiply the number of 0s before a 1 by the number of samples per stride (plus 1 for the base transition), and that's the base duration in samples, which should be divided by the sampling rate to get the number of seconds per base. Sampling rate can be found in the sampling_rate attribute of /???/channel_id.
Some of the signal at the beginning of the read may have been clipped, and it's necessary to take this into account in order to properly align the move table to the signal. The signal offset is found in the attribute first_sample_template for /baseLoc/../../Segmentation_000/Summary/segmentation, and represents the number of samples that should be clipped off the start of the raw signal. There's one more thing that may help for aligning bases to signal, and that's the base offset relative to the move table. The basecaller works in chunks of multiple bases (kmers), so the base corresponding to the move table is actually somewhere in the middle of the called/modeled kmer. ONT doesn't provide any insight into this, so I just try some offsets between 1-5 bases and pick the one that looks the best.

instruction: Calculate the distribution of read lengths from a ~10GB FASTA file with >1M reads of mean length ~8Kb efficiently.
output:
If you want something quick and dirty you could rapidly index the FASTA with samtools faidx and then put the lengths column through R on the command line.

samtools faidx $fasta | cut -f2 $fasta.fai | Rscript -e 'data <- as.numeric(readLines("stdin")); summary(data); hist(data)'

This outputs a statistical summary, and creates a PDF in the current directory called Rplots.pdf, containing a histogram.

instruction: Explain how to reduce ONT sequencing error rate for long-read MHC haplotype assembly, including Illumina error correction and bioinformatics tools.
output: 1. Evaluate the error rate by re‑calling the reads with the latest highest‑accuracy basecaller (e.g., guppy v5.0.11) using the appropriate configuration file for the flowcell (dna_r9.4.1_450bps_sup.cfg for 9.4.1 reads or dna_r10.3_450bps_sup.cfg for 10.3 reads). 2. If no reference is available, perform reference‑free correction using the nanopore reads themselves with Canu: canu -correct -p nonModel -d nonModel genomeSize=10m -nanopore reads.fastq. 3. If a reference is available, use a tool such as Megalodon for reference‑based correction, noting it can also call methylation. 4. To further improve accuracy, especially for small indels, assemble a draft haplotype and use Pilon with Illumina reads; Pilon corrects a reference sequence and is most effective for local indel correction. 5. For MHC haplotyping, reference‑free correction is preferable; apply homopolymer compression to reduce the most common error mode. 6. Use CDHIT to bin similar sequences after compression, facilitating haplotype grouping. 7. For human HLA typing from nanopore reads, similar strategies apply, but perfect base‑level accuracy is not expected.

instruction: Explain whether merging two nanopore sequencing runs that were interrupted by a power loss and completed later will lead to major differences in results, whether it is advisable to merge the runs, and whether poretools is an appropriate tool for merging.
output: It is entirely unclear which type of analysis you aim to do. You probably should be more specific when asking questions. That said, given that the run was going perfectly fine until a power issue stopped it, all data produced should be fine and you can indeed combine those runs. You probably shouldn't bother with poretools. If you are using fastq files to start your analysis you can just cat the basecalled files together. Also after alignment, you can merge the bam files. If your analysis requires the fast5 files then you can probably just put these in the same folder, but you should tell us which analysis you are doing.

instruction: Identify and recommend classifiers that can assign taxonomies to full-length 16S nanopore reads using a full-length 16S reference, and that can work out of the box.
input: MinION, Dada2, Unoise3, Deblur, Phred score 15, read length 1500bp
output: Use Kraken2 with one of the 16S databases. Kraken2 has worked well with nanopore sequences. Pavian can be used to inspect the Kraken2 report files visually. Using uncorrected called reads has worked; any errors from nanopore sequencing should be inconsistent enough that the noise doesn’t matter. Note: if you use the MiniKraken database instead of a comprehensive 16S database, there might be some false positive species assignments depending on which kmers get pruned out. Another possibility is Centrifuge, but it is primarily for metagenomic analysis and may not work for 16S amplicon sequencing.

instruction: Find free open source software tools available for simulating Oxford Nanopore reads.
output:
Simulators designed specifically for Oxford Nanopore:
- NanoSim (https://github.com/bcgsc/NanoSim)
- NanoSim-H (https://github.com/karel-brinda/nanosim-h)
- SiLiCO (https://github.com/ethanagbaker/SiLiCO)
- ReadSim (https://sourceforge.net/p/readsim/wiki/Home/)
- DeepSimulator (https://github.com/lykaust15/DeepSimulator)

General long read simulators:
- Loresim (https://github.com/gt1/loresim)
- Loresim 2 (https://github.com/gt1/loresim2)
- FASTQsim (https://sourceforge.net/projects/fastqsim/)
- LongISLND (https://github.com/bioinform/longislnd)

For an exhaustive list of existing read simulators, see page 15 of my thesis, Novel computational techniques for mapping and classifying Next-Generation Sequencing data (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.1045317).

instruction: Describe how 1D reads are classified as pass or fail in MinKNOW.
output: The classification is based on comparing a mean to a threshold. It uses the mean of underlying quality values prior to Phred transformation. Compute each Phred back to probability, average the probabilities, convert the mean probability to a Phred. The threshold is 7. The mean quality score is stored in the mean_qscore_template column of the sequencing summary file. Sorting by mean_qscore_template shows passes_filter is FALSE for values below 7 and TRUE for values above 7.

instruction: Basecall MinION fast5 data using Albacore v2.3.4 with SQK-LSK109 kit and FLO-MIN106 flowcell via command-line, troubleshoot missing reads, and fix potential multi-read fast5 format issue.
output: You likely have multi-read fast5 files generated by recent MinKNOW versions. Convert them to the older single-read per file format using the conversion script. Albacore does not support multi-read fast5 and is being replaced by Guppy. Use the command: $multi_to_single_fast5 -i /path/to/fast5/ -s /path/to/save/output/

instruction: Identify the cause of the many insertions observed in the alignment of nanopore WGS data to GRCh38 using minimap2.
output: Insertions and deletions are the dominant error mode of long read sequencing, including nanopore sequencing. What you see is not unexpected. Things may have improved by now if you would download the raw fast5 data and repeat the basecalling. There is no need to gunzip the fastq.gz prior to alignment. Your commands for alignment look alright, except that (if you are using a recent version of samtools) you don't really need samtools view and can pipe directly to samtools sort, using -o to specify the output format: minimap2 -ax map-ont -t 20 <index> <fastq> | samtools sort -@5 -o alignment.bam. In a recent version of igv you can right-click the alignment and chose a "Quick consensus mode", which will make your alignments at least a bit prettier. Note how the consensus sequence (top of the graph) is quite clean – while individual reads may be noisy this is mostly randomly distributed and as such ironed out in consensus.

instruction: Describe what the ONT_barcodes_adapters.fa file looks like for use in a nanopore sequencing preprocessing pipeline.
output:
The ONT_barcodes_adapters.fa file contains the public sequences released by Oxford Nanopore Technologies for their sequencing kits, converted into a FASTA format for use in the protocol. It includes adapter and barcode sequences combined into a single file in earlier protocol versions. An example of its contents is:

>ONT_VNP ACTTGCCTGTCGCTCTATCTTCTTTTT
>ONT_SSP TTTCTGTTGGTGCTGATATTGCTGCCATTACGGCCGGG
>BC01 AAGAAAGTTGTCGGTGTCTTTGTG
>BC02 TCGATTCCGTTTGTAGTCGTCTGT
>BC03 GAGTCTTGTGTCCCAGTTACCAGG
>BC04 TTCGGATTCTATCGTGTTTCCCTA
>BC05 CTTGTCCAGGGTTTGTGTAACCTT
>BC06 TTCTCGCAAAGGCAGAAAGTAGTC
>BC07 GTGTTACCGTGGGAATGAATCCTT
>BC08 TTCAGGGAACAAACCAAGTTACGT
>BC09 AACTAGGCACAGCGAGTCTTGGTT
>BC10 AAGCGTTGAAACCTTTGTCCTCTC
>BC11 GTTTCATCTATCGGAGGGAATGGA
>BC12 CAGGTAGAAAGAAGCAGAATCGGA
>RB12A GTTGAGTTACAAAGCACCGATCAG

instruction: Determine the pros and cons of different basecallers in Oxford Nanopore Technology Sequencing and decide which to use for a MinION run on a laptop, considering if you can generate FAST5 files and call bases later.
output: Yes, you can generate FAST5 files and call bases later. Basecalling during the run is useful for quicker results. You can recall FAST5 files with multiple basecallers. The current basecalling options are MinKNOW, Albacore, and Guppy. MinKNOW uses an embedded version of Albacore, but it can lag behind the released Albacore version. Guppy is a GPU‑enhanced basecaller that is not yet released by ONT and is used by default on the GridION; its results are similar but not identical to Albacore and it is much faster with a supported GPU. Metrichor’s cloud basecalling is no longer available, so all basecalling must now be performed locally. You might look at Ryan Wick’s comparison of the different basecallers for more detail.

instruction: Extract a subset of reads from a fast5 archive based on a list of read IDs obtained from a mapping file (SAM/BAM/FASTQ).
output: Create a python program called fast5seek to read the list of read IDs from an input file (FASTQ, BAM, or SAM) and then traverse all fast5 files in a given directory, checking whether each read ID is in the set. If it is, write the file path to an output file or stdout. Use the command pip3 install fast5seek fast5seek -i /path/to/fast5s -r in.fastq in.bam in.sam -o out.txt. If no -o is provided, output to stdout. The output paths can then be piped into other tools, e.g., mkdir subset_dir/; fast5seek -i /path/to/fast5s/ -r in.fastq | xargs cp -t subset_dir/, which copies the fast5 files that match the read IDs into subset_dir/.

instruction: Extract the total number of fastq reads aligning to each amplicon reference from a BAM file generated by minimap2 alignment of nanopore reads.
output:
The quick way to get the number of alignments on each reference is
samtools idxstats my_bam.bam
Number of reads on each reference is column 3. This will give the total number of alignments per reference, not the total number of reads (each read might give rise to more than one alignment). In theory, only one alignment for each read should be marked as primary, so the following should give you what you need quickly and at low memory usage:
samtools view -bF 2304 my_bam.bam > primary_only.bam
samtools index primary_only.bam
samtools idxstats primary_only.bam

instruction: Set the folder where MinKNOW writes raw data on Mac OS X and find the default output directory.
output: To change the output folder, open the user_conf file for your operating system. For Mac OS X, navigate to /Applications/MinKNOW.app/Contents/Resources/conf/user_conf, open the file, locate the output_dirs section, and replace the default path "/Library/MinKNOW/data" with the directory of your choice. The default output directory on Mac is "/Library/MinKNOW/data". For Windows, open Notepad with administrative rights, go to C:\ProgramFiles\OxfordNanopore\MinKNOW\conf, open user_conf, and under output_dirs change the instances of "C:\data" to the desired path (remember to use backslashes). For Ubuntu, go to /opt/ONT/MinKNOW/conf, open user_conf, and under output_dirs replace the default path "/var/lib/MinKNOW/data/reads" with your chosen directory.

instruction: Check if it is safe to use a MacBook Pro for non-intensive tasks such as browsing the web, using Excel, checking emails, or taking notes during a 2-day MinION sequencing run with MinKNOW, and whether locking the screen with Control+Shift+Off will negatively affect sequencing.
output: Yes, it should be fine, especially if basecalling is disabled. Locking the screen is also fine. I prefer to do basecalling after a run to avoid consuming too much processor time and skipping reads.

instruction: Explain what the full_1dsq_basecaller.py/.exe script does and which of the two fastq files should be used for alignment.
output: The 1D$^2$ process has three stages: 1) standard 1D calling of reads, 2) linking similar reads from the same channel in reverse-complement orientation with short pore transition, 3) creating a 2D consensus base call from the two linked reads. The basecaller outputs two files: the 1D calls from step 1 and the consensus calls from step 3. Ideally the 2D consensus calls should be used for assembly; if coverage is too low, the 1D reads can be added. Because linking is not a physical link, 1D$^2$ is not suitable for amplicon reads because complementary reads from different templates may appear consecutively.

instruction: Modify Ensembl D. melanogaster reference transcripts to introduce controlled random base substitution errors without indels, using a tool that can simulate sequencing errors based on an external ONT MinION error profile.
output: Consider read simulators mentioned in the linked question, including those by Karel Brinda. Avoid excluding INDEL errors entirely, as length can be preserved with end adjustment. Note that simulating nanopore reads essentially models the base‑caller rather than the sequencer. For realistic error models, use publicly available data. A relevant study on mouse cDNA sequences is available (bioRxiv 2017) and its Illumina and ONT reads are in SRA SRP082530. No recent D. melanogaster nanopore studies are known; alternatively, you could purchase a MinION RNA starter kit for your own data, but older studies used R7.3 flow cells with higher error rates.

instruction: Find a visualization tool that allows simple examination of splicing of full-length transcripts.
output: IGB provides the intended result without much hassle. It doesn't have the bells and whistles of IGV, but presents a clean and intuitive view of the individual reads. In IGB, each read is presented as a single, contiguous line. If you zoom in you can see the read ID for each read. For a more holistic view, IGV's Sashimi plot can be modified to exclude spurious junctions supported by few reads. The minimum junction coverage can be set by right‑clicking on the Sashimi plot itself, or application‑wide under View → Preferences → Alignments → Splice Junction Track Options. With minimum junction coverage 3, the noisy plot becomes more readable.

instruction: Determine the layout of channels on an Oxford Nanopore flowcell, including how many channels per row and column and how channel numbers map to rows and columns.
output: There are 2048 usable sequencing wells, hexagonally packed with four wells connected to the same sequencing sensor/channel via a multiplex selector. The combination of the mux and channel number determines the physical location of the well. The association between channel number and physical location is not obvious, and within each channel the muxes are also not in an obvious order, e.g. [3,4,1,2,2,1,4,3] for two adjacent channels. Official text description of layout for R7, unchanged for R9.5: Channels 1-64 occur at the top of the chip (other low channel numbers are at the bottom). Channels order down the chip: 1-64, 449-512, 385-448, 321-384, 257-320, 193-256, 129-192, 65-128. Muxes run from left to right in the order: 3,4,1,2,2,1,4,3. The layout matches MinKNOW channel lookup table definitions. An example code to produce image is provided.

instruction: Analyze the quality of alignment (percentage identity, insertion rate, deletion rate) for each of the BAM files.
input: BWA MEM, GraphMap, E.coli K12 MG1655
output:
Qualimap will do this for you.

1. Go to http://qualimap.bioinfo.cipf.es.
2. Run qualimap (default params are fine) on each BAM file.
3. Open up the HTML output, and you can read off the %identity (they measure the opposite, i.e. mismatch rate, but 100% - mismatch rate is %identity of course), indel rate, etc.

One thing to watch out for (you don't mention it in your question, but just in case) is that you cannot directly compare Q scores - these are a bit of a mess and calculated very differently in each piece of software.

Unsolicited suggestion: you might also try NGM-LR for mapping MinION data. We've found it beats the others for our data (though we map to a distant reference).

instruction: Explain the reasons for the difference in yield in Oxford Nanopore sequencing.
output:
- The hardest thing about MinION sequencing is getting the sample in the first place.
- There are lots of different sample types and extraction methods.
- You cannot get longer reads than what you put in; poor sample quality leads to poor output.
- DNA is very stable when not moving, but very sensitive to lateral damage.
- The phenol‑chloroform method of DNA extraction is very good, and can be used with a phase‑locked gel to make extraction easier.
- A simple salt + alcohol extraction might be the best method for extraction (because it involves the least amount of work on the DNA).
- EDTA (e.g. as found in TE buffer, and many extraction kits) is not compatible with the rapid kit.
- The most consistently good Nanopore runs produced by the lab were 1D ligation runs on R9.4; the best overall run was a phenol‑chloroform extraction + rapid kit.
- Getting small numbers of short reads is very important.
- Suggested (and mostly untested) purification techniques: spin column (60‑100 kb); ethanol extraction (100‑150 kb); dialysis (150‑250 kb); low melting‑point agarose plug (~1 Mb, DNA extraction in situ).
- The nanopore protocol input is in nanograms, but should really be stated as molarity; the kit expects about 0.2 pmol input.
- Tapestation, Qubit and Nanodrop are all a good idea; a DNA sample that passes all three tests will work well: no short‑length shoulders by Tapestation, sufficient DNA by Qubit, high purity by Nanodrop.
- RNA can interfere with sequencing; digesting RNA is recommended.
- Freezing DNA is a really bad idea. The ice crystals are very good at chopping DNA up into small pieces.
- DNA that is kept in the fridge is remarkably stable; can be kept for over two years (and probably indefinitely).
- For cDNA sequencing on MinION, the best run produced over 15 M reads.
- Using a PromethION flow cell (P2 Solo) for cDNA sequencing, the best run produced 89 M reads.

instruction: Provide R packages, workflow, tutorials, or guides to identify fungal species from ITS1 and ITS2 amplicons sequenced on MinION long reads.
input: MinION, Oxford Nanopore, ITS1, ITS2, RStudio, fastq, fast5, EPI2ME, NCBI BLAST, Galaxy, fasta
output: Use metagenomics tools such as Centrifuge and Kraken2. The WIMP workflow from ONT, based on Centrifuge, includes fungal sequences from RefSeq. If using NCBI BLAST, split long reads into <5 kb chunks with seqkit (e.g., seqkit sliding -s 4900 -W 4900). In Galaxy, create a workflow with Trim Sequences, FASTQ to FASTA conversion, and NCBI BLAST+. Alternatively, use QIIME2 with the UNITE fungal training set for ITS1/ITS2 analysis. For long‑term use, command‑line tools offer more power and customization.

instruction: Identify software options to demultiplex MinION fastQ files other than porechop, within or outside the Artic protocol.
output: The Guppy basecaller itself now has the ability to internally perform multiplexing. That is currently the preferred option. If for some reason, you still want a stand‑alone application to demultiplex already basecalled reads, then qcat is an option.

instruction: Find an Oxford Nanopore (MinION) sequencing simulator that allows getting fast5 as output, or provide alternatives.
input: Oxford Nanopore MinION sequencer, fast5, NanoSim
output: Simulation of MinION output does not make sense when there are many freely available FASTQ and FAST5 sequences for testing. Real FAST5 files can be obtained from resources such as Clive Brown’s genome (ONT-HG1) under a research‑only license, the nanopore‑wgs consortium NA12878 data under a CC‑BY license, mouse parasite data, E. coli data, or a sample FAST5 file of sensor noise from a Dropbox link. Always acknowledge the data source.

instruction: Determine if the MinION fastq files are 2D forward and reverse and find a way to download a 2D equivalent without downloading the fast5 files.
output: Probably not. ENA doesn't have any ability to specifically flag a file as 2D, and the nanopore file formats have changed a lot, so you pretty much need to rely on the submitter to separate out the reads in a meaningful way. Also, there wasn't really any consistent convention for what ONT used in their read headers. Looking at some of my old files they had a plain sequence ID (i.e. without anything suggesting temp/comp) in different template/complement folders in the FAST5 files for the components of a 2D read and the consensus sequence, but later changed it to _2D_template and _2D_complement as in the example you have (possibly with an intermediate _1D_template and _1D_complement format). Parsing/understanding those FAST5 files can be difficult, which is why poretools was created in the first place. It's still a bit of a mess, with the 1D$^2$ reads ending up in a Frankenstein-like state that's partially 1D and partially 2D.

instruction: Use poretools to extract FASTQ from fast5 files and explain why the output may be empty; describe the difference between basecalled and non-basecalled fast5 files.
output: No, poretools does not do basecalling. The poretools fastq command can be used to extract the FASTQ information from the basecalled FAST5 file (via MinKNOW live-basecalling or albacore). Alternatively, both of these basecallers can export a FASTQ file directly if desired. The difference between a basecalled FAST5 and a nonbasecalled FAST5 is the presence of the FASTQ within the file. This can be directly viewed using an HDF5 viewer, like h5dump from the commandline, or HDFView from the GUI.

instruction: Determine whether any two non-identical 20bp CRISPR guide RNA sequences in the GeCKO v2 set differ by fewer than three bases (less than 15%) and how to identify such pairs.
input: MinION, 20bp, GeCKO v2 set, dimer and trimer counts
output:
130k * 20bp is a small data set. At this scale, SSE2 Smith-Waterman may work well:


git clone https://github.com/attractivechaos/klib
cd klib && gcc -O2 -D_KSW_MAIN ksw.c -o ksw -lz
./ksw -a1 -b1 -q1 -r1 -t14 20bp.fa 20bp.fa > out.tsv


I simulated 130k 20bp reads from E. coli K-12. The last command line takes about ~2 CPU hours based on the partial output.

It is possible to make this much faster by using 5bp seeds. You can also try ssearch and fasta from the fasta aligner package. They are slower, probably because they are not optimized for such input. Computing Hamming distance with SSE2 will be much faster, but is probably of little use as it does not allow gaps. There are also Gene Myers' edit-distance based algorithms, which can be faster than SSE2-SW.

Bowtie2 (as well as most short-read mappers) won't work well at the default setting. Bowtie2 uses long exact seeds. It will miss many 1-mismatch hits, let alone 3-mismatch ones. The number you get from bowtie2 is an underestimate. You might be able to tune bowtie2, but to find 3-mismatch hits, you have to make it a lot slower.

instruction: Determine whether it is possible to sequence Oxford Nanopore MinION offline without a special arrangement and explain Oxford Nanopore Technologies' policy towards third‑party base‑callers.
output: Short answer: yes, but you need to get permission and modified software from ONT before doing that. Sequencing on MinION uses ONT software MinKNOW and requires firmware that is usually downloaded from ONT servers at the start of a run; therefore you normally cannot sequence without server access and ONT knowing about it. ONT acknowledges that some users lack internet access (e.g., sequencing in Africa or on the ocean) and a support email can lead to a local software fix. Once raw signals are acquired, base‑calling can be performed locally. ONT’s maintained basecaller Albacore is local, receives updates, is open source but has restrictive licensing; ONT also offers a free and open source basecaller nanonet that will receive the same updates. Other non‑ONT basecallers exist, such as deepnano. All basecalling repositories released by ONT are under the Mozilla Public License, with Scrappie included as a testing/bleeding‑edge version of Albacore.

instruction: Decide between PacBio HiFi and Illumina sequencing platforms for aChrysina scarab genome to identify color pattern loci and perform phylogenetic analysis, and explain why adding short reads to polish HiFi assemblies may lower quality and introduce more errors.
output: HiFi reads can resolve most repeats. Illumina reads from repetitive regions may be mismapped, creating consensus errors. Short‑read polishing tools such as Pilon tend to reduce contig consensus quality. In a non‑inbred diploid, HiFi reads provide extensive local phasing, whereas Illumina reads usually cancel or discard that phasing, yielding a worse, less informative assembly. In theory, if Illumina reads are used correctly, they can improve HiFi consensus as shown by a haploid/inbred pipeline, but no generic tools exist for typical genomes. Thus the facility’s advice is largely correct: avoid polishing HiFi assemblies with Illumina reads.

instruction: Find PacBio HiFi reads that contain a specified sequence (7kb or 1.5kb) and output either the read ID or the full read sequence.
output:
I have seen the blasr aligner typically used for aligning PacBio reads to a reference. Is there a reason why blasr would not be a good choice? I guess the very short sequence you are trying to align to might be an issue.  

Minimap2 is capable of mapping pacbio reads, and can also do pairwise mapping to see if reads overlap with each other, so this function might help you: From https://github.com/lh3/minimap2 

minimap2 -x ava-pb reads.fq query_seq.fq > ovlp.paf

instruction: Determine whether Illumina pair‑end reads can be used to polish a PacBio assembly with Pilon in the presence of high heterozygosity.
output: Resequencing will not reduce heterozygosity. You can use Pilon plus short reads to correct the PacBio assembly even with a highly heterozygous sample. Use the --diploid option in Pilon to indicate that there are heterozygous sites. Pilon corrects heterozygous SNPs and indels differently; it performs best with SNPs and sometimes does not correctly identify heterozygous indels. Pilon does not handle phasing; it will correct using the reads in the BAM file, producing a mosaic of the dominant allele at each heterozygous site, which may be influenced by Illumina coverage bias.

instruction: What quality score encoding does PacBio use now?
output: PacBio uses PHRED 33, but the newer PacBio Sequel Sequencer reports all base qualities as PHRED 0 (ASCII '!'). The RS-II reports PHRED 33 quality scores. The Sequel provides data in the form of subreads, which are the consensus sequences from a single zero-mode waveguide (ZMW). The base quality for every base is '!' equating to a PHRED quality of 0. PacBio reports an overall read quality of 0.8 as a placeholder and is working to make this information more readily available.

instruction: Explain how to relax the mapping stringency criteria for minimap2, including relevant parameters and alternative approaches.
output:
Use the short‑read preset ("-x sr") for minimap2. Heng Li suggests trying:

minimap2 -axsr -A2 -B4 -O4,24 -E2,1

If the tool is still not sensitive enough, reduce the parameters -k and -w (reducing -w has less impact on speed, while reducing -k increases sensitivity). He notes that stampy is an older alternative, and that BLAST can provide richer low‑stringency mapping options, though it is slower. Other metagenomic aligners are also available.

instruction: Explain why fewer alignments occur when using the -c flag with minimap2 and determine which alignment is preferable.
output: The program's author pointed me to the Minimap2 FAQ: 1. Alignment different with option -a or -c? Without -a, -c or --cs, minimap2 only finds approximate mapping locations without detailed base alignment. In particular, the start and end positions of the alignment are impricise. With one of those options, minimap2 will perform base alignment, which is generally more accurate but is much slower. So, to specifically address the main questions asked here: 1) Why are there fewer and non-identical computed alignments when the CIGAR tags are added? minimap2 alignment accuracy differs when running minimap2 -k15 -w10 vs minimap2 -c -k15 -w10. It is actually not unexpected that the alignment coordinates in each PAF file are only similar, not identical, and an increase or decrease in the number of alignments between the outputs is simply an artefact of this accuracy discrepancy. 2) Which alignment (default vs. with CIGAR tags) is better or preferable? Although both alignments report very similar sequence relationships, adding the -c flag for CIGAR tag generation is preferable to get more accurate base alignments.

instruction: Determine which columns of a minimap2 PAF file indicate the genomic coordinates where a gene target mapped, and how to use them to extract the corresponding sequences.
input: minimap2, sort, awk, seqtk subseq, BED
output: You may have used the query as the reference; they should be switched. A minimap2 mapping command is normally: minimap2 reference.fa query.fa > results.paf. In the PAF output the first column is the query sequence name and the sixth column is the target sequence name, which is the opposite order of the command line. For mapping a gene target to a genome you should run: minimap2 genome_contigs.fa GENE_TARGET.fa > results.paf. After this, columns 8 and 9 will represent the genomic coordinates that the gene target mapped to. Minimap2 matches each query to a reference, typically producing one primary mapping per query; switching the order changes this behavior. The PAF columns correspond to the closest sequence ID to the left of them, ordered opposite to the command line order.

instruction: Explain the purpose of indexing a reference genome with minimap2 before mapping, whether it speeds up mapping, and whether minimap2 automatically generates an index if none is provided.
input: minimap2, -x preset, -d target.mmi target.fa, -a, target.mmi query.fa, target.fa query.fastq, .mmi
output: If no index is supplied, minimap2 creates one on the fly, so it is done automatically. This makes the mapping step slower, but index creation is fast (a few minutes for a human genome), so the impact is minor. Creating the index separately does not speed up mapping unless you use that index multiple times.

instruction: Determine whether minimap2 can map sequences shorter than 32bp and suggest alternative mappers if necessary.
output: Minimap2 is designed for long reads and may not map very short sequences effectively. You could try reducing the minimizer k‑mer length (e.g., with the –k option, default is 15) and/or the minimizer window size (with –w). Alternatively, for reads shorter than 32 bp use bwa aln followed by bwa samse, and then merge the resulting alignments.

instruction: Determine if adapter removal/trimming is needed for 10x single-cell sequencing raw data from bcl2fastq, and if R1 reads require further processing.
output: Just use cellranger. The data after the cell barcode and UMI in R1 is garbage and cellranger knows how to handle it.

instruction: Find an alternative source for fastq_illumina_filter or a replacement tool.
output: I found this site after Googling fastq_illumina_filter and comparing the file name to that shown in an archived version of your link: https://www.sigenae.org/drap/install.html. Direct link to tool tarball: http://genoweb.toulouse.inra.fr/~ccabau/fastq_illumina_filter-0.1.tar.gz. This seems to work.

instruction: Find a C or C++ tool that quickly and efficiently counts the number of reads and bases in a compressed FASTQ file.
output: Use Heng Li’s FASTQ Parser in C. Download the example tarball from the provided link, modify the example code, compile it with make, and run the resulting program on the .fastq.gz file. The program reports the number of sequences and the total number of bases. Performance measurements show it runs in about 49 seconds for a 35m-read, 75bp file, which is comparable to the zgrep/awk approach.

instruction: Explain whether reads from sample 3-1 could be misassigned to sample 6-2 or reads from sample 1-1 could be misassigned to sample 8-2 due to index hopping, and whether these results are connected.
output: The best way to mitigate index hopping for short reads is to use dual/unique barcodes on each end of a sequenced library for each sample, as is done here. Index hopping is thought to result from concatenated reads caused by occasional ligation of multiple template sequences during adapter ligation, rather than just ligating adapters to template DNA. In a typical read the left barcode (L1) and right barcode (R1) belong to the same sample. Concatenated read possibilities, such as L1<seq1>R1<seq2>R2, can produce a read that appears to have the left barcode of one sample combined with the right barcode of another. This leads to an unknown barcode that is a combination of two sample barcodes, and the abundance of such unknown barcodes will be higher when the contributing samples are abundant. The most abundant unknown barcode in this case could be formed from the left barcode of sample 8-2 combined with the right barcode of sample 3-1, producing a read pair that appears to have the barcode sequence GTCCTAAG+GTTAAGGC. This mechanism explains how reads from one sample can be assigned to another sample due to index hopping.

instruction: Split the fastq files into five smaller files, clustering reads by the sequence types seq1, sub1, sub2, sub3, and sub4 based on their shared segments.
output: Use seqkit locate with the start or end of the CDS sequence to get a tabulated output of CDS locations in each read; assign reads to one of the five types based on these locations. Optionally use clumpify.sh from BBMap to cluster reads before searching for CDS locations. For more accurate grouping, extract regions around the deletions with flanks (15–20 bp) into a fasta file and run seqkit locate. Run the command: zcat input.fq.gz | seqkit locate --threads 8 --max-mismatch 0 --pattern-file fasta_with_5_frags_extracted_from_algnt.fa > result.tsv. Then create separate files for each fragment: grep frag_name_1 result.tsv | cut -f 1 > frag_name_1.read_names.txt; seqkit grep --pattern-file frag_name_1.read_names.txt input.fq.gz -o frag_name_1.reads.fq.gz. Repeat for all five fragment names.

instruction: Are perfect phylogeny and maximum parsimony phylogenies the same?
output: No. A perfect phylogeny is one such that all characters evolve on the tree with no homoplasy, i.e. for a binary character, changes occur once from 0 -> 1 but never from 1 -> 0. Maximum parsimony inference may produce a perfect phylogeny, but typically for real datasets some degree of homoplasy is required to explain character patterns.

instruction: Provide an aggregation of robust single-individual sequencing resources for non-human, non-mammalian metazoan species.
input: Genome in a Bottle, Illumina WGS, Oxford Nanopore, PacBio, 10X reads, pre-aligned bam, verified variant calls, SRA
output:
In addition I would like to add "Vectorbase", the curator "Dan" is a good guy (I forget his surname right now) and it's a good team. However, please read my rather somber assessment of arthropod genomics first.

We appear to be talking about lower metazoans, e.g. not protists, or yeasts (the latter I suspect are really good for your question). I don't know anything about worms.

Its important to understand that arthropods are several (soccer) divisions lower than mammals in terms of the question you are asking, except for two model organisms.

Flybase, Drosophila melanogaster is the most important species for your purposes. The second most important species within the Class Insecta will be Anopheles gambiae (malaria mosquito).

Personally I would stick to the main players because overall whilst there are a lot of insect genomes (because there are a lot of insects), their genomes remain rough because of the difficulties in assembly and requirement to physical mapping their genomes in comparison to their genome size. Also except for D. melenogaster the (incredible) tardiness in producing a draft genome. For example the idea of PacBio is in its infancy for many entomologists: and this would help them a lot in assembling their genomes correctly. I agree things have picked a lot recently, but I remain a skeptic.

Within the arthropods I would be very surprised if the crustacea, myriapods or Chelicerata (e.g. spiders) were in better condition than hexapods.

If you are interested in protists certainly Plasmodium falciparum (African malaria) has the sort of information you seek (Plasmodb).

Summary I don't want to discourage you, insects are great, but you need to be selective if you are from a human/mammalian genetics background.

instruction: Use Picard CollectInsertSizeMetrics on a single-end BAM file and determine how to tell Picard that the data is single-end or find another tool that can estimate insert size for single-end data.
input: Picard CollectInsertSizeMetrics I=Ctrl_sorted.bam O=Ctrl.insert_sizes.txt H=Ctrl.insert_sizes.pdf M=0.5 MAX_RECORDS_IN_RAM=2000000 VALIDATION_STRINGENCY=LENIENT, warning: All data categories were discarded because they contained < 0.5 of the total aligned paired data
output: CollectInsertSizeMetrics collects insert size metrics by parsing the TLEN field from a BAM file. It is intended only for paired-end data, not single-end, because single-end reads do not have a TLEN value. For targeted assays such as ChIP‑seq, the macs2 peak caller includes a module that estimates fragment size based on strand cross‑correlation. If the data is your own, ask the laboratory personnel who prepared the library to look up the size from a Bioanalyzer or TapeStation quality control. If the data is from a publication, check the methods text for the procedure or kit; most NGS assays have a relatively standard insert size depending on the exact library preparation.

instruction: Determine the line number of a specific read in a FASTQ file and replace a base at a given position using sed.
output: You'll first need to determine the appropriate line number, which you can do with grep -m1 -nw "@31027" foo.fastq. After that, note that you can provide a line number to sed: sed -i '123456s/CAAAAGTCACTCA/CAAAAGTCACTTA/' foo.fastq. That will do the replacement only on line 123456 and edit the file in place (the -i option).

instruction: Extract all sequences from a FASTA file that start with a specified 5' region of 43 nucleotides, allowing for fuzziness in that region so that any matching sequence of any length can be written to a new file.
output:
Here is a quick python3 solution that requires BLASTN and biopython. The results are output in FASTA format to stdout. 1. Save the following code to hit_finder.py:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
from Bio import SeqIO
from Bio.Blast.Applications import NcbiblastnCommandline

cline = NcbiblastnCommandline(query=sys.argv[1], subject=sys.argv[2], outfmt="'6 qseqid length qstart'", max_hsps=1, word_size=4)
result = [j[0] for j in [i.split('\t') for i in cline()[0].rstrip().split('\n')] if int(j[1]) >= int(sys.argv[3]) and int(j[2]) <= int(sys.argv[4])]
for record in SeqIO.parse(sys.argv[1], 'fasta'):
    if record.id in result:
        print(record.format('fasta'))
2. Make the script executable: chmod +x hit_finder.py.
3. The script takes four arguments:
   - arg1: query_fasta_file (file to search in)
   - arg2: subject_fasta_file (database file to contain the sequence to match at 5')
   - arg3: min_alignment_length (length of the hit, allows mismatching in string with blastn and a word_size of 4)
   - arg4: start_of_hit_at_5prime (where the alignment hit should start)
4. Example usage:
   • To search for AATCTGTAGTACGACTCACTATAGC in query sequences, save the sequence in subject.fa:
     >subject AATCTGTAGTACGACTCACTATAGC
   • Store query sequences in queries.fa.
   • Run:
     ./hit_finder.py queries.fa subject.fa 23 1
   • To search for hits of at least 30 bp no more than 10 bp from the 5' end, run:
     ./hit_finder.py queries.fa subject.fa 30 10
   • To save results to a file, redirect stdout:
     ./hit_finder.py queries.fa subject.fa 30 10 > results.fa

instruction: Align a large number of 120-300 bp reads (100,000-1,000,000) to a 1500 bp reference sequence efficiently, possibly using a suitable algorithm or workflow.
output: Use a whole genome aligner like BWA or Bowtie2, replacing the whole genome reference with your 1500 bp sequence. These aligners can align large numbers of NGS reads to a single reference, even if the reference is short or a small virus. For local alignment, run Bowtie2 with the --local flag, and run BWA using the BWA-MEM mode.

instruction: Explain why a variant appears with the reference and alternate alleles seemingly switched and how to avoid false positives to keep only novel and rare true positive variants.
output: The variant callers are correct: the reference allele is C and the alternate allele is G, although G is the common allele in the population. The ref/alt are not switched. No error occurred. To avoid false positives, you can build or use a database of such common variants and filter them out, or check if the reference genome has been updated (e.g., 1000 Genomes or T2T).

instruction: Provide bioinformatics evidence that would debunk the current conspiracists about coronaviruses.
output:
The scenarios are impossible and would be laughable if they were not so serious. The evidence is in the phylogenetic trees. It is a bit like a crime scene when the forensics team investigate. We have done enough crime-scenes often going to the site, collecting the pathogen, sequencing and then analysis – (usually neglected diseases) without any associated conspiracy theories.

The key technical issue is coronaviruses are zoonoses, pathogens spread to humans from animal reservoirs and phylogenetic tree really helps understand how the virus is transmitted.

Trees
1. The key thing about all the trees is bats. Bats lineages are present at every single point of the betacoronavirus phylogeny (tree), both as paraphyletic and monophyletic lineages. One example is this tree of betacoronaviruses. Meaning the nodes connecting the branches of the tree to the "master-branch", represent common ancestors and these were almost certainly bat-borne coronaviruses. This is especially true for SARS and bat-viruses are EVERYWHERE.
2. The tree also shows that SARS arose independently on two occasions, again surrounded by bat lineages and 2019-nCov has emerged separately at least once, again associated with bats.
3. Finally the tree below is a figure from BioRxiv Zhou et al (2020) "Discovery of a novel coronavirus associated with the recent pneumonia outbreak in humans and its potential bat origin" shows the 2019-nCov lineage is a direct descendant of a very closely related virus isolated from a bat (RaTG13*). This is a really conclusive finding.

Note, I don't normally present inline images, but it is such a nice finding (hint to reviewers) and BioRxiv is open access.

Conspiracy theory 1: laboratory made virus
Literally it would require someone passaging a new virus, with unknown human pathogenicity, and independently introducing all the earlier passages en masse across bat populations of China. They would then hope each lineage becomes an independent virus population before, then introducing the virus to humans. Thus when field teams of scientists go around using mist nets to trap the bats, buy them from markets, isolate the virus and sequence it they would find a beautiful, array of natural variation in the bat populations leading up to the human epidemics, that perfectly matches vast numbers of other viral zoonoses. Moreover, this would have to have happened substantially prior to SARS and 2019-nCov, because the bat betacoronaviruses have been known about prior both epidemics, viz. it is simply not feasible.

Biological explanation General
Bats are a reservoir host to vast numbers of pathogens, particularly viruses, including many alphaviruses, flaviviruses, rabies virus and believed to be important in ebolavirus (I don't know about this) and even important to several eukaryotic parasites. It makes sense, they are mammals, so evolutionary much closer to us than birds for example, with large dispersal potential and roost in 'overcrowded' areas enable rapid transmission between bats.

Technical
The trees show bats are the common ancestor of betacoronaviruses in particular for the lineage leading into the emergence of 2019-nCov and SARS, this is seen in this tree, this one and the tree above. The obvious explanation is the virus circulates endemically in bats and has jumped into humans. For SARS the intermediate host, or possible "vector" was civet cats.

The theory and the observations fit into a seamless biological answer.

Conspiracy theory 2: Middle Eastern connection
I heard a very weird conspiracy theory attempting to connect MERS with 2019-nCov. The theory was elaborate and I don't think it is productive to describe here.

Biological explanation
All the trees of betacoronaviruses show MERS was one of the earliest viruses to diverge and is very distant from 2019-nCov, to the extent the theory is completely implausible. The homology between these viruses is 50%, so its either MERS or 2019-nCov. Its more extreme than mixing up yellow fever virus (mortality 40-80%) with West Nile virus (<0.1%), the two viruses are completely different at every level.

What about errors?
Phylogeneticists can spot it a mile off. There are tell-tale phylogenetic signatures we pick up, but also we do this to assess 'rare' genetic phenomina. There is nothing 'rare' about the coronaviruses. The only anomaly is variation in the poly-A tail and that is the natural variation from in vitro time-series experiments. Basically we've looked at enough viruses/parasites through trees, that have no conspiracy theories at all (often neglected diseases), and understand how natural variation operates - so a phylogeneticist can shift the wheat from the chaff without really thinking about it.

Opinion
The conspiracy theories are deeply misplaced, and the only connection I can imagine is its China. However, the Chinese have loads of viruses, influenza in particular which causes major pandemics, but that is a consequence of their natural ecology (small-holder farming) allowing the virus to move between reservoir hosts. I've not visited small-hold farms in China, but I have in other parts of the world and when you see them, you get it. The pigs, chickens (ducks … China), dogs, horses and humans all living within 10 meters of each other.

Conclusion
Shipping large numbers of bats to market, bat soup, raw meat from arboreal (tree-living) mammals such as civets that are sympatric to bats. Then consider the classical epidemiology in light of the phylogenetic data, which is very consistent, a single picture emerges that coronavirus is one of many zoonoses which has managed to transmit between patients.

Summary
The fundamental point is the bioinformatics fit into the classical epidemiology of a zoonose.

Note
The bat coronavirus RaTG13 predates the 2019-nCov outbreak by 7 years. It is not even clear whether the virus has been isolated, i.e. could just be a RNA sequence.
"They have found some 500 novel coronaviruses, about 50 of which fall relatively close to the SARS virus on the family tree, including RaTG13—it was fished out of a bat fecal sample they collected in 2013 from a cave in Moglang in Yunnan province."
Cohen, "Mining coronavirus genomes for clues to the outbreak’s origins" Feb, 2020 Science Magazine.

instruction: Explain whether using the '-t illumina' option is acceptable for Illumina MiSeq reads, and suggest recommended values for '-q' and '-l'.
output:
It'd be rare to run into Illumina encoded quality scores any more, I doubt there are machines running that still produce those. Everything produced at the moment uses Sanger encoded (phred + 33) scores.
There is no optimum value for -q and -l, though reasonable values would typically be <=5 for -q and ~5 for -l.

instruction: Simulate paired‑end reads for each sequence in a FASTA file using the coverage values specified in a text file.
output: I am not aware of any software that can do this directly, but I would split the fasta file into one sequence per file, loop over them in BASH and invoke ART the sequence simulator (or another) on each sequence. For more information about ART, please see their paper here: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3278762/

instruction: Read an SCF file in Python using sangerseqR via rpy2.
output:
It was done using sangerseqR with rpy2 in python

import rpy2.robjects as r
from rpy2.robjects.packages import importr
utils = importr('utils')
utils.install_packages('sangerseqR', repos="https://git.bioconductor.org/packages/sangerseqR")
utils.chooseBioCmirror(ind=1) # select the first mirror in the list
utils.install_packages('sangerseqR')
sangerseqR = importr('sangerseqR')
Trace = sangerseqR.readsangerseq( file="1I1_F_P1815443_047.scf") #scf sequence file is now loaded

sangerseqR Documentation(pdf)

instruction: Find public databases that provide Sanger chromatogram data in Ab1 or SCF format for large-scale download, suitable for machine learning.
output: The NCBI Trace Archive is a permanent repository of DNA sequence chromatograms (traces), base calls, and quality estimates for single-pass reads from various large-scale sequencing projects, containing more than 2 billion traces.

instruction: Find the index sequences present in the Undetermined_S0_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz file and provide their frequencies.
output: Check the DemultiplexSummaryF1L1 file in the output folder. In that file, locate the section titled "Most Popular Index Sequences". It lists index sequences along with their hit counts and reverse complements, for example: TGACCA with 2455964 hits, CGATGT with 2320579 hits, GGCCCC with 2023652 hits, CGCGGG with 1983605 hits, and so on.

instruction: Determine whether supplementary alignments should be considered when calculating variant allele frequency for a specific position in Illumina short read data edited with CRISPR-Cas9.
input: Illumina short reads, Crisp-Cas9 editing, pysamstats, GATK HaplotypeCaller, Mutect2, supplementary alignment
output: It depends on the tool and use case but generally they should be counted. With short reads, supplementary alignments are uncommon so the impact is small. For long reads, they are more frequent and excluding them would artificially lower allele counts. Unless you suspect they are artefacts from editing or mapping, include them. You might apply QC, such as checking that multiple reads support the supplementary mapping and assessing quality.

instruction: Check which software was used to generate the BAM file.
output: The information may be in the BAM header, in lines starting with @PG, which record the command lines used. Run samtools view -H your.bam to show the header.

instruction: Explain why TLR4 is annotated far from the peak and whether it is an issue with the code or data, and suggest a solution.
input: ChIPseeker, TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38.knownGene, clusterProfiler, org.Hs.eg.db, annotatePeak, tssRegion
output: The tssRegion argument defines what is considered a promoter. ChIPseeker does not filter the annotated peak file. In the results there is an annotation column that indicates the genomic region the peak falls in. If it falls within the tssRegion window it will be labeled as a promoter. In this case the annotation is 1st Exon (ENST00000413759.1/ENST00000413759.1, exon 1 of 1) 9. This means the peak is not in the defined promoter region but in the first exon of the transcript. The peak is actually far from TLR4 (374699 bp), but nearby sequences are pseudogenes, so TLR4 is chosen as the closest gene. You can view the annotation overview with plotAnnoPie(peakAnno). tl;dr: you need to filter the results of the ChIPseeker annotation to get the genomic regions you want.

instruction: Explain why the 2019-nCoV genome sequence on NCBI uses ATGC letters instead of RNA letters.
output: That is the correct sequence for 2019‑nCoV. Coronavirus is an RNA virus and in fact, every RNA virus in GenBank is present as cDNA (AGCT, i.e. thymine) rather than RNA (AGCU, i.e. uracil). The reason is simple: we never sequence directly from RNA because RNA is too unstable and easily degraded by RNase. Instead the genome is reverse transcribed, either by targeted reverse transcription or random amplification, and thus converted to cDNA. cDNA is stable and is essentially reverse‑transcribed RNA. The cDNA is either sequenced directly or further amplified by PCR and then sequenced. Hence the sequence we observe is the cDNA rather than RNA, so we observe thymine instead of uracil and that is how it is reported.

instruction: Count the number of reads in sample.bam that support the variant chr22 425236 C T and the number that do not, on Linux.
output: samtools mpileup -f reference.fa -r chr22:425236-425236 alignments.bam will produce output where you can count the bases for that position. You could use the command line to do most of that automatically: samtools mpileup -f reference.fa -r chr22:425236-425236 alignments.bam | cut -f 5 | tr '[a-z]' '[A-Z]' | fold -w 1 | sort | uniq -c. That will give a count of how many of each base were seen.

instruction: Identify and explain the discrepancy between samtools flagstat output and Picard MarkDuplicates metrics regarding duplicate read percentages for NIPT samples sequenced on Illumina Novaseq.
input: bwa mem, picard MarkDuplicates, samtools flagstat
output: There are duplicates, as shown by the line '1636809 + 0 duplicates'. This gives a duplication rate of 1636809/26595942 ≈ 0.0615. According to the samtools flagstat documentation, each line shows counts for each category in the format '#PASS + #FAIL'. Usually you use the first column to compute percentages.

instruction: Explain why different bases in a read should follow the same pattern of allele frequency in a fastqc Per Base Sequence Content plot.
output: The positions you see in the x‑axis of this plot are positions on your reads, not on the reference genome. Since each read comes from a random position on the genome, the frequency of A/T/C/G on each base position should reflect the base composition of the entire genome. Therefore, the percentage of each base should be approximately constant across the read.

instruction: Create a single FASTA sequence from the given BAM, VCF, and reference FASTA files using consensus generation.
output:
Use bcftools to generate a consensus VCF and then create the consensus FASTA:
$ bcftools mpileup -Ou -f ref.fa input.bam | bcftools call -Ou -mv | bcftools norm -f ref.fa -o output.vcf.gz
$ bcftools consensus -f ref.fa output.vcf.gz > out.fa

instruction: Explain why only a fraction of NGS reads contain adapters after sequencing and trimming.
output: Sequencing begins at the end of the adapter, so adapters are only visible when the read sequence extends through the entire fragment into the adapter on the other side. If the fragment is long enough, no adapter will be seen, which is the intended result of the library preparation.

instruction: Export an image of the aligned reads that includes all reads, not just those currently shown in the window, using IGV.
output:
Run an IGV batch script to increase the number of reads per image. Create a script file with one command per line, for example:

snapshotDirectory ./
goto chr2:28582094-28582790
maxPanelHeight 10000
snapshot chr2_28582094_28582790.png

Run the script via Tools > Run batch script. The key parameter is maxPanelHeight 10000, which allows more reads than the default snapshot. You can also use a shell script to send the commands to IGV via telnet, for example:

#!/bin/bash
mkdir -p /tmp/igv
sleep 2
echo "snapshotDirectory /tmp/igv"
sleep 2
echo "goto chr2:28582094-28582790"
sleep 2
echo "maxPanelHeight 10000"
echo "snapshot chr2_28582094_28582790.png"
sh foo.sh | telnet 127.0.0.1 60151

Open IGV, load the BAM file, and run the script.

instruction: Explain why flow cells from the same experiment might be run weeks apart, assess potential batch effects, and describe how to check for batch effects when merging sequencing files before downstream analysis.
input: fastq.gz, flowcells, merge, trim, mapping, counting
output: Sequencing libraries are usually built per sample and barcoded; they are pooled into a library pool that can be stored and reused. A pool can be sequenced multiple times by sampling it, and each run sequences a subset of the molecules. Sequencing runs are highly repeatable and typically do not introduce batch effects. To check for batch effects you should analyze each sequencing file separately before combining them. Also verify whether the same library pool was used for all runs or if different pools/libraries were made for each run; separate pools increase the risk of batch effects.

instruction: Explain how to identify heterozygous indels in a chromatogram and distinguish them from noise, including considerations for homopolymer regions.
output: If you want to call heterozygous SNPs and INDELs, you may try polyphred. I believe there are a couple of other similar tools, but I have forgotten their names. Your two points are correct. Basically, at some position, you start to see two streaks of overlapping peaks. Once identified, the two streaks can be aligned to each other if you shift one of them. This is a strong constraint, but it is hard to tell by eye because it is not obvious how to assign peaks to streaks without running an automatic tool. Doing this is even more difficult given noisy traces. I think you'd better run polyphred or similar. The INDEL mutation rate in long homopolymer runs is orders of magnitude higher than elsewhere in the genome. This has two implications: 1) you expect to see more homopolymer indels; 2) PCR polymerase is more likely to introduce errors in homopolymers––a higher fraction of homopolymer indels tend to be wrong.

instruction: Determine what index sequence HiSeq reads for a library with IS4 p5 oligo and explain how MiSeq and HiSeq machines read index sequences.
output: Illumina machines read the two indices in separate index sequencing reactions where they have sequencing barcodes specific for indices. If you have no index, then you should either not bother with creating either index read or mask that during demultiplexing. If you're mixing indexed samples with an unindexed sample, you can't really rely on any particular sequence being returned by the machine (i.e., just use the "undetermined indices" fastq file). The unindexed adapters tend to not have the full index sequencing primer binding site on them, so reads without index generally don't return much signal. In practice, they're likely to adopt barcode sequences of neighboring clusters. We see that a lot when we spike in PhiX.
